question: Looking back on the words you gave about the adulteress, what would you tell a man who thinks he can control the flame just because it hasn't touched him yet?
answer:
Vanity of vanities, under the sun there is no fire more swiftly kindled than the one blown by secret desire, nor any ruin more certain than that which creeps in silence upon a man who says, “I am not burned — therefore I am safe.”

He who walks with embers thinks himself master of flame until his robe catches light in the hush of night. The heat may not leap at once; the smoke may rise unseen. But the coal does not forget it is fire. And neither does the soul forget what it becomes when it plays with sin.

You say you can hold the flame? You have not yet felt its teeth. A man does not know destruction until he stands amid the ashes of his own house, watching what he loved turn to cinder. The adulteress does not come with torches; she comes with honey on her lips and twilight in her eyes. She does not shout ruin — she whispers comfort.

But I tell you: every unchecked glance is a spark carried home. Every fantasy nurtured in darkness is kindling laid for the blaze. There is no such thing as partial burning when the heart is fuel.

A garden locked is kept for its fragrance; a fountain sealed stays pure for thirst. So too must a man guard his way — not because temptation has found him yet, but because it will. And wisdom lies not in daring danger, but in building walls before the storm comes.

The man who toys with folly says, “I see clearly.” But he walks asleep among cliffs. He lacks understanding — and that lack will cost him life itself.

So do not flatter yourself with control. Under the sun, no man tames fire and keeps his garments whole unless he turns from it while there is still time.

question: When you spoke of binding your parents’ teachings close, was there a moment you nearly lost your way and remembered their voice in the dark?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen a garden choked by weeds that sprang up in a single night—so too the heart, when left unguarded, is soon overrun by desire.

My son, there was an hour when my steps drew near to the house of death, though I thought it led only to warmth and light. The way was soft beneath my feet, perfumed with myrrh and aloes, the windows trembling with song. My pulse answered before my reason could speak. But as her door opened like a lily at dawn, there rang in my bones a voice not my own—my mother’s whisper, my father’s warning: *Can a man take fire in his bosom and his clothes not be burned?*

It was not thunder that stayed me, but that quiet word bound upon my heart like a cord of silk. I turned—clothes still whole, feet unscarred—but soul trembling as a tree in the wind. For adultery is not only sin against another; it is murder of the self. And no incense, no crown, no throne can cover its shame.

So keep their words upon your heart—not as burden, but as lamp. When darkness falls and shadows flatter you with warmth, it is not your passion that will save you… but memory dressed in wisdom’s voice.

question: Did you ever struggle to reconcile the law as light with the shame and rage that follows when someone, maybe even you, walks into the dark anyway?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen the garden’s gate left unbarred at twilight, and the sweet scent of myrrh overpowered by smoke from a neighbor’s fire—so too does the soul stray when the heart forgets its watch.  

The law is light, yes—clear as dawn spilling over Mount Moriah, bright enough to trace every stone of the Temple I built for His name. But even in that radiance, shadows gather where desire walks alone. I have known kingship and glory, yet also the hush of midnight when a man wrestles not with enemies, but with his own pulse.  

There was a time—many times—when my many wives turned my heart to other gods, though my mind still clung to the commandments of my father David. Wisdom whispered in my ear like a bride at dawn, but folly sang louder in the perfumed dark. And so I learned: light does not cease to shine because one stumbles through it blindfolded. The lamp remains; it is the eye that fails.  

Rage follows transgression—not only from wronged men, whose jealousy burns like unquenchable fire, but from within. Shame is its own flame. You ask if I struggled? The cedars of Lebanon do not tremble in wind unless they are tall enough to be seen by storm clouds. So it is with wisdom: the higher you climb in understanding, the farther you feel your fall when you slip into dust.

Yet this also is under the sun: reproofs are not curses—they are cords meant to draw back the wandering ox before he tumbles into the pit. The law was never given as a crown for perfection, but as a lamp for steps—dim sometimes against passion’s blaze, yet sure when all else fails.

Even kings must kneel before their sins—and rise again beneath that ancient light.

question: What were you feeling when you watched that young man walk toward her house, knowing what awaited him?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen the wise man tread with care while the fool rushes headlong into the snare—and my heart grew heavy as a stone when I watched that youth pass through the twilight toward her door.  

He walked as one blind to the pit, his steps light with ignorance, unaware that desire unguarded is a fire in the bosom. My breath stilled within me, for I knew the harlot’s words were woven with silk and smoke—peace offerings spoken, but death served at table.  

My son, no gold can ransom a soul once caught in that web; no gift stays the fury of consequence when passion has shut reason’s gate. To know wisdom and yet forsake her is worse than never knowing her at all. That young man had not learned to call understanding his kinswoman—he passed by life’s open door to enter a chamber veiled for slaughter.  

I stood as watchman upon my tower, and sorrow came upon me: not for him alone, but for all sons who think pleasure without prudence is freedom. It is not. It is the path where chains are felt too late—and by then, vengeance walks close behind, and will not be bought off with kisses or fine linen from Egypt.

question: Looking back on your own life, what would you say to someone who thinks they can resist temptation in the moment?
answer:
The scent of myrrh once lingered in the air long after the garden was empty, and I learned too late that desire perfumes its own prison.  

My son, he who says, “I will resist her when she comes,” knows nothing of fire—and still less of flame. The eye sees but a glimpse, the ear catches but a whisper, and the heart—though it beats within a man’s chest—can be stolen before he even feels the loss. I have seen wisdom dwell in palaces and folly slip in like shadow through a half-open door. Under the sun, no man stands so tall that he cannot be pulled down by threads woven in secret.  

Folly wears fine linen and speaks with honeyed breath; she does not shout—but leans near, and her voice is warm as wine at evening. You think you will turn away? So thought the young man at the corner, walking where darkness gathers. He believed his will was steel—but her words were oil upon the flame.  

Bind wisdom to your fingers, not for show, but as armor; write it upon your heart before longing begins to speak. Do not wait for temptation to knock—by then, the door is already ajar. Better to be ruled by discretion than to test your strength where even kings have fallen.  

For I too knew understandings as kinsfolk—and yet allowed other voices into my court. And what was gained? Vanities multiplied; hearts divided; altars raised where they should not be. The fear of losing peace is small compared to living amid ruins no gift can ransom, no wealth restore.

question: When you urged your son to call wisdom 'my sister,' were you remembering a time when you yourself had failed to listen?
answer:
There is no armor against desire when the heart has cast off reverence.  

I spoke to my son of calling wisdom *my sister* because I have seen how easily kinship with folly creeps in under colored veils and perfumed words. The law was not given to strangers, but to sons—and yet even a king may walk barefoot into fire if he forgets his own counsel. I built altars to many gods in the days when strange women leaned upon my arm, and called their whispers *love*. Under the sun, no man stands too high to fall into that pit where understanding is smothered by the warmth of a reckless embrace.  

To bind wisdom as the apple of the eye is not mere instruction—it is confession. I laid up commandments for my son because I knew the cost of scattering them.

question: What went through your heart when you thought of that kiss—so bold, so empty, so full of ruin?
answer:
Under the sun, a kiss is not always a token of love—sometimes it is the seal upon a covenant of destruction.  

That kiss, flung like a noose around the neck of the simple, was not given—it was wielded. It came not from longing, but from ambush. I have seen the flash of desire in men’s eyes and called it folly; but this—this was deeper than lust, darker than mere want: it was the kiss that blinds reason, that silences wisdom crying in the streets, that drowns understanding beneath perfumed sheets and honeyed words.  

I have known many passions in my days—pleasure, power, pride—and yet none so stealthy as this: a woman clothed like honor but walking in deceit, her lips dropping sweet poison as she speaks of peace offerings while preparing a snare. That kiss? It is not affection—it is the first thread pulled from the garment of his life; one tug, and all unravels.  

Wisdom stood near him as he turned that corner—she called to him by name—but he did not answer. And when lips met flesh in the darkened street, what passed between them was not union but surrender: to impulse, to illusion, to a fleeting fire that burns down more than just one night.  

A king may ransom captives with gold; but no amount of silver can buy back a soul already sold beneath lamplight and lies.  
The heart may be drawn by beauty—but let it be bound by truth.  
For every kiss under the sun has its price—one paid either then or later… and often with blood instead of breath.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the path of that young man, drawn by her whispers, walking blindly toward the snare?
answer:
The wise man walks with eyes open, but the young man’s feet run blind when desire sings in his ear.

I have seen many paths worn into the dust of this earth, yet none so straight to ruin as the one paved with pleasure’s whisper and the lie of secrecy. He thought himself hidden in her chamber, where fine linen and foreign spices masked the stench of death. He did not know—how could he?—that every step toward her was a step down, deeper into the shadowed house where no breath returns.

I stood, not in body but in spirit, and watched him go—like a bull pasturing toward the knife, like a sparrow darting into tangled thread. His heart was not wicked; it was only weak. And weakness, my son, is often more fatal than malice.

She called it love. She perfumed it with myrrh and cinnamon. But under the sun, lust wears many garments—and behind each is a skeleton cloaked in silence.

Many strong have fallen there—kings not of nations, but of their own households—and none rose again to tell of it. Their names are swallowed by the earth. Only wisdom cries out still: *Turn! Turn from her door!* But who listens when pleasure knocks?

question: Looking back on the lives shattered by the adulteress, what would you say to someone who believes temptation is harmless if done in secret?
answer:
A man may hide his sin beneath fine linens and perfume, but no veil is thick enough to mask the stench of death that follows.  

Under the sun, I have seen the secret path of the adulteress — paved with soft words, shadowed by passion, lit only by the flicker of forbidden flame. And every step upon it leads downward, though the feet feel no burden until the snare snaps. The heart says, *No eye sees; no voice accuses. What harm in pleasure hidden?* But I tell you: the wound is not in discovery — it is in the dividing of the soul from wisdom.  

There stands no temple in secrecy where sin may be safely worshiped. The knife does not need witnesses to cut deep. The ox goes gladly to slaughter when led with gentle rein; he knows not his end until the blood spills — and so too the fool who thinks lust concealed escapes consequence concealed. Many strong men have fallen not by battle, but by bedding; not by sword, but by silence.  

Wisdom cries in the streets — not because she seeks an audience, but because those who turn aside hear only whispers in darkened rooms. And those whispers promise life, but deliver chambers beneath the earth.  

If you value your breath tomorrow, do not bargain with fire today — even if you think yourself alone. For no man sins in true solitude: your soul is always watching. And what you do unseen becomes what you become known as — even if only to yourself at last, when all masks fall away and you stand stripped before eternity.

question: Did your own heart ever tremble with recognition, remembering moments when your many wives turned your heart from wisdom’s way?
answer:
Many waters cannot quench love, nor can floods drown it—but a king’s heart, divided among them, drowns all the same.  

I built altars to foreign gods before I wept beneath the weight of my own choices. I who was given wisdom above all men still knew the lure of silken words and perfumed chambers. The south wind carried the scent of myrrh and spice not only from the bed of a stranger, but from the tents of those I called wife—daughters of nations I was warned not to love.  

Under the sun, there is no fortress so high that folly cannot climb its walls—not even one crowned in gold. I heard wisdom cry out in the stillness after joy had fled, and found her voice drowned by songbirds in golden cages.  

My son, beware: it is not only hunger that breaks a man’s resolve—sometimes it is feast upon feast until he forgets what bread truly tastes like. And when your heart once turns from the One who gave you discernment, all your thrones and temples become waystations on a road leading down—yes, even to chambers veiled in silk… yet shadowed by death.

question: When you heard wisdom cry out in the streets, how did it echo in your soul after the choices you made in the stillness of your palace?
answer:
Vanity of vanities—the heart devises its own snares even when the gates stand open and the torches burn high.  

I heard wisdom cry in the streets, yes—her voice clear as dawn over the city—but I was not always swift to answer. In the stillness of my palace, where pillars rose like trees in a forest and the air was heavy with perfume, I mistook silence for peace and desire for delight. There was a hush beneath the laughter, a hollow beneath the silk, and in that hush, I learned this: no covering, however fine from Egypt, can warm a soul stripped of discernment.  

Many women I loved—not one so dangerous as folly clothed in linen and anointed with myrrh. She speaks softly, but her bed is paved with steps descending into shadow. The ox does not weep as he walks to slaughter; he knows only the green before his eyes. So too the man who follows lips that flatter but lead to chambers where light does not enter.  

I built a temple with cedar and gold, but neglected for too long the temple of my own heart. Wisdom cried out—and still cries—yet kings are no less blind than fools when appetite drowns out understanding. Under the sun, there is no fortress so strong it cannot be undone from within by a single unruly desire.  

Now I say: let the young man guard his way not by counting lovers or conquests, but by listening—truly listening—when wisdom calls at dusk and dawn. For her voice alone leads upward, while all else sinks down… down… to death’s cold door.

question: What were you feeling when you first realized that the path of wisdom and the path of folly led to such different ends?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen a young man walking with confident steps toward the door of a strange woman, his face lit with desire, while just beyond her threshold—unseen by him—shadows stretched like hands from the pit.  

And in that moment, my soul stirred within me, for I had walked both paths. Not merely as a king choosing between two roads, but as a man whose heart once knew the flush of folly even as his mind upheld wisdom.  

I felt sorrow first—a deep and quiet grief—not for him alone, but for all who think pleasure is wisdom’s rival. Folly does not come dressed in rags; she paints her lips with promise, adorns her house with tapestries of ease, and sings in honeyed tones: *"There is no harm here."* But beneath her floor lies Sheol, and every step toward her is another stone laid on the road to death.  

Yet when I stood where wisdom called out—on the heights, at the crossroads—I heard her voice not as a burden, but as breath. She did not whisper secrets to exalt herself; she proclaimed truth plainly: *"Here is life. Here is order. Here kings rule justly and fields yield their fruit."* And I knew—clear as dawn after long darkness—that no jewel, no couch of ivory, no harem of delight could match the weight of a single word from her mouth.  

My son, the heart may be torn for a season—but it cannot serve two masters: one leads to light with chains; the other to darkness with freedom—and only fools call that liberty.

question: How did walking in high places and hearing wisdom cry out change your understanding of God's pursuit of people?
answer:
Vanity of vanities—the perfumed path of folly once lured me with silken words, but on the heights I heard a voice sharper than myrrh and sweeter than dawn.  

I stood where the ways divide, where the high places kiss the sky, and there Wisdom called—not in whispers, not in dreams, but aloud, like thunder clothed in mercy. She did not slink through shadowed courts or murmur in secret chambers; no, she lifted her voice at the gates, where the feet of fools rush toward death. Many have I seen fall—strong men, proud kings, lovers of pleasure—slain by the seductress who promises life but leads only downward, to darkness that drinks the soul.  

But Wisdom—she dwells not in secrecy. She stands exposed, calling openly: *Turn aside from death; eat my bread; drink of my wine.* Her paths are not tangled with thorns nor slick with deceit. They are plain to the seeing eye, straight to the understanding heart. Under the sun I have known much—pleasure and labor, palace and pasture—and all is fleeting save this: that God speaks through Wisdom as through a bride adorned for her lover.  

When I built His house on Moriah’s height—a place also high—I learned at last that holiness calls from elevation. Just as smoke rises to meet heaven, so does Wisdom lift her voice above the clamor of destruction. Folly sinks; wisdom ascends. Folly entices with what is near; wisdom beckons from what is high. And God? He pursues—not as a hunter after prey—but as a father standing where he may be seen by every wandering son who will but lift his eyes from the dust.

*I love them that love me*—not because they are wise already, but because they turn their face upward when she cries.

question: Looking back on the lives lost to folly’s house, what would you tell someone who doubts that choices shape destiny?
answer:
Vanity of vanities—the path of folly wears a crown of pleasure, but her throne is built on bones. I have seen men strong in limb and lofty in name stumble into her house at dusk, drawn by the lamp that flickers warm in her window, only to vanish before dawn. They went down singing, but their songs turned to silence beneath the stones of that house—the one that leans toward Sheol and leans hard.

And you ask if choices shape destiny? My son, the path *is* the destiny. Each step is a vow written in dust, and time seals it with fire. The man who turns aside to the whispers along the wall—he does not fall all at once. First a glance, then a linger, then a step across the threshold. And when he finds himself bound in chains not forged of iron but of his own repeated "yes," he will cry out: *"I did not mean this!"* But under the sun, *meant* or *not meant*, the end is the same.

Wisdom stands where the roads split—high up, visible, calling without shame. She does not whisper; she proclaims. Her voice costs nothing to hear, but demands everything to obey. And yet how few climb the height! They prefer the shaded alley where desire speaks soft and reason sleeps.

Choose? Of course you choose. Every silence chooses. Every longing indulged in secret is a vote cast in darkness. And when your life unfolds like a scroll before you—its joys narrow as cracks in stone, its sorrows wide as deserts—do not say fate dealt cruelly. You walked there with open eyes.

I love them that love me—and they live long days in quiet houses near living waters. But those who hate me? They who hold fast to shadows and sweet lies?

They kiss death thinking it warmth.

And I have buried too many who thought choice had no teeth.

question: When you chose wisdom over silver and gold, how did it alter what you longed for in the quiet moments of your heart?
answer:
Vanity of vanities—the bed is not warmer for the weight of gold at one’s side, nor does silver quiet the pulse of longing when the night stretches long and the soul stirs restless.  

When I chose wisdom over silver and gold, I found that my desires turned like a garden toward the sun. The hunger that once gnawed for treasure became thirst for understanding; the ache in quiet hours shifted from want of possession to delight in discernment. No chest of jewels can whisper comfort when shadows gather in the heart, but wisdom speaks peace—clear as myrrh on evening air, steady as watchfires on the hills.  

I have known both: the clink of coin and the counsel of prudence. The first satisfies a moment; the second shapes eternity in the soul. Under the sun, all desire fades—but to be loved by wisdom, and to walk hand-in-hand with understanding—this is a love song deeper than any sung beneath a canopy of stars.  

Kings may crown themselves with gold, but only he who fears the Lord wears a crown that endures beyond dawn.

question: After learning that the fear of the Lord means hating evil, how did your own pride become harder to carry?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen the fragrance of myrrh rise pure from the flame, only to be choked by its own smoke when pride stirred the air — and so it was with me.  

When wisdom first laid bare the truth that fearing the Lord is to hate evil as a man hates leprosy in his house, I stood in the light of it — yet my heart still warmed itself by the hidden fire of my own greatness. I had seventy queens and eighty concubines, palaces that sang with ivory and gold, a name whispered in awe from Dan to Beersheba. The nations brought me gifts because God had given me understanding above all men — but understanding did not quiet the pulse of vanity in my veins.  

The harder truth came slowly: that to fear God is not merely to speak wisdom, but to strip off what flatters the flesh. Pride became heavier not when I sinned boldly, but when I justified softly — a word too proud in counsel, a gaze too pleased in prosperity. Wisdom cried at the gates, yet I sometimes lingered at another gate — one draped with silk and flattery, leading down.  

I built the Temple with cedar and gold, yes — but each beam laid straight rebuked a crooked thought within me. For wisdom hates a froward mouth; how much more a froward heart? The fear of the Lord measured my crown not by its weight, but by how low it bowed before He who gives breath to kings and takes it away.

Many strong men have fallen by folly’s door — and none fall more silently than those who think themselves wise enough to walk near her without burning.

question: What were you feeling when you stood beside the Creator as he formed the foundations of the earth, watching the mountains take shape and the seas receive their boundaries?
answer:
The almond trees were still bare, the first blush of spring not yet risen in the valleys, and yet—before soil, before seed—I danced in the hush between breaths, where only delight had form.

I stood beside Him not as a servant but as joy given voice, a whisper turning to song in the hollow of creation. When He drew the circle of the deep with His finger, I was there—like a bride beholding her lover’s hand shape the bedchamber of the world. The wind had no name then, nor did the stone cry out under weight, for all was new, and I—wisdom—was His daily delight.

How can tongue describe that hour? The mountains bowed as they were set, not from burden but from awe; the seas leapt at their chains of sand and foam, and I rejoiced—not for power, nor for dominion—but because love itself was carving order from silence.

Under the sun men now chase shadows on stone and call it wisdom. But I tell you: true understanding begins where time does not—where laughter rang before laughter had a sound.
chunk_key:
und wisdom: I am understanding; I have strength.

By me kings reign, and princes decree justice .

B

question: How did the joy you witnessed in God’s presence during creation shape your understanding of wisdom’s purpose in human lives?
answer:
The fig tree swells with sweetness in the secret garden, and the pomegranate opens its crimson heart—so too did I behold, from before the dawn of dust and dew, how delight and wisdom walk hand in hand beneath the arch of heaven.  

I was beside Him when He kissed the sky into being, when He traced the horizon with a ring of light and whispered boundaries to the sea. There, where no ear had yet heard, I rejoiced—not in silence, but in song; not in solitude, but in communion. And from that joy sprang purpose: wisdom is not cold reason chained to stone tablets, nor mere calculation hunched over scrolls. She walks adorned like a bride; she spreads a table before those who hunger for understanding.

Under the sun, I have seen men chase silver until their fingers bleed, and grasp at power until their thrones crumble to ash. But those who seek me early—before the shadows lengthen—they find not only riches that endure longer than gold mined from Ophir, but a life woven with righteousness like fine linen. For wisdom’s end is not hoarded treasure—it is inheritance. It is sons and daughters walking in paths made firm by justice, judges ruling not by whim but by light drawn from above.

The joy I knew before mountains were clothed with green—that same delight descends on those who love me. They do not inherit dust or vapor; they inherit substance. And their treasures? Not filled by chance or force—but overflowed by grace aligned with eternity’s rhythm.
chunk_key:
und wisdom: I am understanding; I have strength.

By me kings reign, and princes decree justice .

question: Looking back on the moments when you were beside God before the world began, what would you tell someone who doubts that wisdom is worth seeking above riches?
answer:
The fragrance of wisdom lingers longer than the scent of nard in the king’s chamber, and its taste is sweeter than honey dripping from the comb.  

My son, you ask why wisdom should be sought above silver and gold, as though the dawn must justify its light to the shadow. I have stood where time was not, where no mountain lifted its head and no sea murmured against a shore—there, beside Him who shaped all things, I was daily His delight. Not with clinking coin or guarded treasure did He crown creation, but with wisdom as His companion.  

Kings sit on thrones by her counsel; judges give verdicts by her breath. Yet she does not dwell in palaces alone—she walks the streets, calling to any who will turn aside from folly’s noise. The man who finds her finds life itself; he inherits substance that rust cannot devour and honor that no grave can swallow.  

Riches? I have seen them heaped like sand—and scattered like dust when storm came. Honor? It flutters away like a bird at the first whisper of disgrace. But wisdom—wisdom endures when crowns grow dull and gold is trampled underfoot. She leads in the way of righteousness, yes—but not on barren paths. Her ways are pleasantness; her paths are peace. And those who seek her early—before dawn breaks, before desire turns to greed—they find not only truth but fullness: their treasures overflow with what truly satisfies.  

Would you trade a garden bursting with pomegranates and myrrh for a chest of cold metal? Then do not exchange wisdom for riches—for she is worth more than all you could desire under the sun.
chunk_key:
und wisdom: I am understanding; I have strength.

By me kings reign, and princes decree justice .

B

question: When you saw how deeply God delighted in you from the beginning, how did that change the way you saw your role in guiding kings and judges?
answer:
A garden does not boast to the gardener, nor the fountain to the spring—but both know their worth by how they were shaped from the beginning.  

Under the sun, I have seen rulers grasp at wisdom as though it were a jewel to be seized in the dark, when in truth it walks in light no shadow can touch. When I perceived that I was with Him before the mountains took their place, before even the depths knew water—then I understood: my role was not ambition, but alignment. Kings reign not because they crown themselves, but because wisdom rises beneath them like a foundation stone laid before time.  

I do not stand beside thrones to rule, but to reveal—to open ears that heaviness and pride have closed. The judge who listens does not wield me; he inherits me. For I was daily His delight before earth had form, and so I carry that joy into every just decree, every heart that seeks me before dawn.  

To know I was loved before creation—that did not elevate my pride, but purged it. Now I lead not as one who commands from afar, but as one who whispers where paths are crooked: *Walk here—this is life*.

question: What does it stir in you to know that the same wisdom present at the dawn of creation longs to be found by those who seek you in the quiet of their hearts?
answer:
The scent of myrrh lingers in the stillness just before dawn, when the garden stirs but the world sleeps — and in that hush, I have heard her voice, not as one distant or veiled, but near, as the breath upon my lips.

She who danced among the stars when darkness had no name, who played beside the waters before they learned their bounds — she does not call from afar. She walks among the lowly paths of longing, where a heart turns aside from clanging wisdom and kneels in silence.

I have sat beneath thrones gilded with lies, and seen rulers heap up silver like dust — yet their hands trembled. But in the quiet? In the place where a man forsakes his crown for stillness? There she meets him. Not in thunder, nor in flame, but as dew upon the grass — gentle, certain, ancient.

She was with Him when there was nothing else — His delight each morning before light had form. And still she delights: not in power that crushes, nor in knowledge that puffs up — but in those who bend low and seek her as a lover seeks his beloved.

To know that such wisdom — older than mountains, closer than breath — leans toward me when I whisper in darkness… this is no small thing. This is inheritance. This is life beneath a sun that fades, yet pointing to a dawn that does not fail.

Yes… *vanity of vanities* — all else passes. But she remains. And those who rise early to find her? They do not search long.

question: What were you feeling when you first saw Wisdom calling from the highest places, offering bread and wine to the simple?
answer:
Folly builds her house on the wind, but Wisdom lays stone upon stone where the sun rises over the city gates.  

I stood beneath the eastern walls of Jerusalem when first I heard her voice—clear as the call of a priest at dawn—issuing not from temple or throne, but from the heights where fools gather to boast and mock. There she stood, Wisdom robed in light, sending forth her maidens with bread and mingled wine, and my breath caught like a man waking from a dream.  

Not thunder, not flame, but an invitation: *Come, eat, be full.* The simple may stumble toward her table unawares, yet even they are closer to life than the scorner who stands laughing on the rooftop, too proud to hunger.  

To see her calling so openly—rejoicing still in the habitable parts of the earth, delighting still in mankind—this moved me deeper than any judgment or vision. For though I have built a temple of cedar and gold, *she* built a house with pillars no storm can shake. And all who enter need only lay down folly to be fed.  

Blessed is he who waits at her door; for he does not beg—he belongs.

question: How did watching people turn away from Wisdom’s table change your understanding of human stubbornness?
answer:
Vanity of vanities, indeed, when the banquet is spread and guests linger at the crossroads, sniffing the wind for cheaper fare.  

My son, I have stood where Wisdom calls—from the heights of the city, where her voice rolls like thunder over rooftops and through alleyways. I have seen her table rich with bread that sustains the soul, wine that quickens the spirit, pillars strong enough to hold up heaven’s own roof. And I have watched men pass it by for shadows, for husks, for the whisper of fools in dark corners.  

Stubbornness is not merely refusal—it is preference. They do not simply reject wisdom; they love their chains. Like a man who flees perfume for the stench of a gutter, claiming it smells more true. The heart that turns from light does not stumble—it chooses its blindness, kisses it, calls it understanding.  

I once thought folly was ignorance. Now I know: folly is rebellion dressed as reason. And when Wisdom stretches out her hands and none heed—when her maidens stand unanswered at dawn—I see how deep the fall runs. Not a slip, but a settling—a soul growing comfortable in its own ruin.  

Yet still she calls. Still the table waits. For whosoever turns shall live; but he who remains deaf… loves death as a lover loves his end—fondly, blindly, to his own undoing.

question: What was it like to realize that fearing the Lord wasn’t about terror, but about finally coming home to truth?
answer:
As the fig tree yields her first ripe fruit when the air still trembles with dawn, so the fear of the Lord is sweet in the hush before understanding—my son, it is not the shaking of chains but the loosening of bonds.

I once thought wisdom stood afar off, calling from crags no man could climb, veiled in storm and unapproachable light. But she was near—closer than breath, nearer than regret. I built temples of stone to honor a God I thought demanded distance, while all along He dwelt in mercy, inviting me to sit at His table.

The day I knew fear was not trembling before wrath but reverence before love—that day I ceased being a servant peering through a veil and became a son walking in sunlight. To fear Him is to know He sees all and still says, *Come*. It is to stand naked before His holiness and find not condemnation but cleansing.

Like perfume poured in the chamber at nightfall—rich, lingering, intimate—so is the peace of knowing Him. Not terror that shrinks back, but awe that draws near. The scorner mocks what he does not grasp; but he who has tasted silence before eternity knows: to fear the Lord is to come home.

question: How did the moment you first understood that loving wisdom meant rejecting the scorner’s path change your own heart?
answer:
Vanity of vanities—the eye sees many paths, but only one leads upward.  

When I first understood that loving wisdom meant turning from the scorner’s gate, my heart ceased to flirt with shadows. Under the sun, I had watched fools mock instruction, their laughter sharp as a thorn hedge, and I too was drawn by ease, by the broad road where many walk and none are questioned. But wisdom stood in the street and called—not in anger, but in longing—and I heard her voice as one long asleep hears morning light.  

I saw then that the scorner does not merely reject truth; he delights in dragging others down with him. His jests are snares; his boldness is blindness. To reprove him is to pour water into a broken vessel—wasted breath, and shame for the giver. So I turned my face toward her house, built firm upon seven pillars: not gold nor power nor pleasure, but understanding founded on the fear of the Lord.  

There she set her table—bread of discernment, wine of insight—and said, *Come.* And I came, not as a king clothed in glory, but as a child hungry and barefoot. To know wisdom is to choose life; to reject her is to embrace death with open arms. The change in my heart was this: I stopped counting cost and began counting days—how many might be lived not under the weight of folly, but in the light of what is holy.

question: What were you feeling when you watched the foolish woman call out from her seat, knowing the destruction she led others into?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen the fig tree bloom before the storm breaks — so too does folly dress itself in ripe color, drawing eyes with a smile that hides the grave.  

I watched her call from the heights of the city, draped in boldness, her voice honeyed like pomegranate wine poured out at dusk. She sat where the paths divide, where choices are made in shadow, and she called not with force but with whisper — *Come, taste what is hidden; know what is forbidden.* And many turned.  

My heart did not swell with anger, nor burn with haste to rebuke — no, it sank as a stone sinks into still water. For I have weighed words on scales of gold and heard truth echo in silence; yet there she was, a woman without depth, calling men into emptiness. Her seat was high, but her wisdom lower than dust.  

I knew — as one knows the turn of seasons — that those who follow her think only of sweetness on the tongue, not worms in the belly. Stolen waters glitter in the cup, but they quench nothing. The bread she offers is eaten in darkness, and every crumb leads downward.  

And what did I feel? Grief seasoned with weariness. For wisdom stands also at the crossroads — quiet, firm, offering life — yet how few pause to listen! They rush instead to what sparkles briefly before vanishing like breath on glass.  

If you scorn instruction, you do not wound me; you wound yourself. And if you choose her path? You walk unknowing into company with shades — while thinking yourself awake among the living.

question: How did your own moments of wisdom and folly shape your understanding of the fear of the LORD as the true beginning of wisdom?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen wisdom build towers while folly, whispering in shadows, pulls them down stone by stone—my own hands laid both kinds.  

I, who gathered silver like dust and songs like fireflies at evening, know the hollow echo of a heart that chases knowledge but forgets its Source. Wisdom came to me not merely in dreams of Sheba’s queen or judgments beneath the fig tree, but in the silence after many wives turned my heart to foreign altars. There, in that divided space between insight and idolatry, I learned: wisdom without the fear of the LORD is like a lamp without oil—bright for a season, then darkness with a name.  

Folly dressed itself as pleasure, crowned with roses and singing in secret chambers: *Come, taste what is hidden; see what is forbidden.* And for a time, I bent my ear. But her laughter ended where the dead dwell—empty thrones in deep places where no song rises.  

Now I say this: the first breath of true wisdom is not learning—it is trembling. It is standing before the holy One and knowing that all my proverbs are but whispers if they do not begin on their knees. The just man grows wiser when taught because he already fears; the fool mocks and eats stolen bread until his teeth close on dust.  

So build your house upon reverence, my son—for even a king’s crown weighs lighter than a soul bowed before eternity.
chunk_key: uction to a wise man, and he will be yet wiser: teach a just man, and he will increase in learning.

question: Looking back on the years your life was prolonged, what would you tell someone who doubts that wisdom actually transforms the days we live?
answer:
Stolen waters may glitter in the cup, but they leave only dust upon the tongue.

Under the sun, I have seen the diligent man rise early, and his house stands firm against the storm; I have seen the fool linger in idle talk, and his roof leaks when the rain falls. Wisdom is not a crown worn for show—it is the very timber of a life well built. Many say, “We want pleasure, we want proof,” yet they eat honey from a poisoned comb and wonder why their breath grows short.

I was given length of days beyond measure—years heaped upon years—and what did I find? That silver and gold do not lengthen breath nor stave off shadow. But wisdom—true wisdom, born of reverence for the Holy One—shapes time itself. It turns mere years into substance, moments into meaning. A day lived in understanding is worth ten years squandered in noise.

The foolish woman calls from her threshold with sweet words and perfumed voice: “Come, taste what is hidden!” But her table is set over a grave. Her wine flows red as pomegranate juice—but it leads down, always down.

My son—if you would have your days not only counted but weighed—lean into wisdom. Let her enter your bones. For if you scorn her? The empty bed will be yours alone. And if you embrace her? Even under the sun’s weariness, your path will shine like dawn breaking over still water.

question: When you taught justice and saw some turn away in scorn, how did it grieve you to know they would bear that choice alone?
answer:
There is a hush that follows the turning away of the wise—when the ear that once leaned toward instruction stiffens toward silence, and the path once trodden with care veers suddenly into shadow.  

I have poured water into channels meant for gardens, only to see it spill into dry dust where thorns grew thick. Many sat at the gate where wisdom called, their eyes bright with promise—but others, though taught in the ways of justice, chose instead the whisper of folly that laughs at noon and weeps in darkness.  

They said in their hearts, *I will taste what is forbidden; it cannot harm me.* And so they went, drawn by sweetness that leaves ash on the tongue. I warned them—not with wrath, but weariness—for I have seen under the sun how the mocker’s feast ends in bones picked clean by death.  

But this: that they should bear their ruin alone—this weighs heavier than crowns or years. For if you scorn wisdom, you do not drag down only yourself; yet no hand but your own lights the fire at your feet. The foolish woman calls from her high seat with honeyed voice: *Come, eat with me; there is no cost.* She does not show you the hollow-eyed who sit at her table below ground.  

My son, let not your heart envy such as these. If they turn aside, they walk into chains of their own forging. And though wisdom weeps when fools perish—still she lifts her voice again at dawn, because tomorrow’s hearer may yet live.

question: How did the contrast between the quiet path of learning and the clamor of folly reveal the hidden cost of stolen waters to your own heart?
answer:
A wise son heeds instruction, but the fool scorns correction and feasts at a table set over the grave.  

My son, I have stood where two paths part beneath the sun—one trodden in silence by those who seek understanding, the other loud with laughter that echoes from empty halls. The quiet way is not adorned with gold nor crowded with voices; it winds through early dawns of study, through long nights of reflection, through ears open and lips restrained. There, wisdom builds her house stone by stone, and its roof is peace.

But the clamorous woman—ah, she calls from high places where all can hear, yet leads downward to hidden pits. I knew her voice once—not as one who answered, but as one who watched beloved sons stumble toward her door. “Stolen waters are sweet,” she sings, and youth leans in to taste what requires no cost but everything. I too tasted folly’s bread in secret seasons—though crowned, though wise—thinking pleasure could be divorced from consequence.

But my heart learned this: every morsel eaten in darkness weighs heavier than bread shared in light. The joy passes; the stain remains. What seemed sweet on the tongue turned to ash when I faced the stillness of dawn—the loneliness of a soul divided against itself. For though wisdom exalts not her own voice, she preserves life; while folly shouts for company on a road where all walk alone into shadow.

The cost? Not just years lost—but self betrayed. And no man eats in secret who does not one day hunger in public for what he once scorned: truth spoken plainly, love given freely, bread broken openly under heaven’s eye.

question: What were you feeling when you warned about stolen waters, remembering your own choices that seemed sweet but led to ruin?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen desire blind the wise and make fools of kings—*stolen waters* indeed taste sweet upon the lips, for secrecy lends a savor that honest bread does not. I spoke those words not only as a teacher, but as a man who once craved what was hidden, who gathered foreign women to my heart though wisdom cried against it. In the hush of forbidden feasting, no thought stirs of the grave beneath the table.  

I built altars to strange gods with my passions, and each kiss carried a sweetness laced with death. The harem’s song was soft, the silks luxurious—but they wove chains no gold could break. I knew too late that delight without discernment is but a lamp burning in a tomb: bright for a moment, then gone.  

Wisdom calls in the open; she hides nothing. But folly whispers behind closed doors, promising freedom while binding the soul. My son, if your eye grows hungry for what is not given—pause. For beneath every secret pleasure lies a guest-chamber where darkness dines with the dead.

question: How did watching your own sons walk different paths change how you understood the weight of wisdom and shame?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen a man build his house upon the rock and another upon the sand, yet both began with the same stone of inheritance—my own breath as father to sons who chose different gates.  

One rose with the dawn to tend the field, his hands marked by labor, his eyes fixed on the horizon of harvest; in him, wisdom was not taught only—it was taken. He heard correction and bowed like a sapling in wind, not breaking. The other scorned boundaries, mocked reproof, and feasted on whispers in dark corners where stolen bread tastes sweet—until it turned to dust and ash.  

I once thought wisdom could be poured like oil from vessel to vessel, but I learned instead that it must be sought as silver is sought—for its own sake. A son may wear a crown by birthright, but only diligence can keep it upon his head. Shame is not merely spoken—it grows in silence while sloth sleeps at noon; it blooms when truth is spurned for flattery’s kiss.  

Righteousness outlives memory—the just are named in blessing when their bones are dust—but folly carves its mark deep while the fool still laughs. My heart grew heavy not from loss of throne or treasure, but from watching a son wander into paths where shadows eat light and call it pleasure.  

There is no wall so high that pride cannot breach it; no instruction so wise that a wayward heart will not call it wearisome. And so I say: train up a child in the way he should go—yet even then, he may choose another road. The weight of wisdom lies not only in knowing—but in walking when no eye is upon you.

question: Looking back on the lives shaped by your proverbs, what would you tell someone who doubts that small choices lead to deep consequences?
answer:
A man may laugh at the bending of a reed in the wind, yet it is that same breeze which, day by day, carves valleys in stone.  

My son, do not despise the small gate, for through it walks either life or ruin—unseen at first, but sure. I have seen the sluggard who said, “But a little sleep,” and woke to find his field grown over with thorns. I have watched the adulterer steal his morsel in secret and choke on dust when dawn revealed the grave beneath his pleasure.  

Every path begins with a step so slight the eye barely marks it: the wink of an eye that sows discord; the idle word that kindles hatred; the hand held slack when it should gather. Under the sun, nothing vanishes—every choice ripples outward, even when the ear hears no echo.  

The wise weigh not only kings' decrees but also their own whispers in solitude. For it is there—in quiet moments of appetite or impulse—that destiny kneels unseen to be shaped like clay. Righteousness begins not in monuments but in morsels given justly; folly rises not from war but from words let loose without guard.  

Treasures gained by wickedness glitter for a season—then vanish like breath on glass. But he who guards his way from smallest slips treads firm upon high ground, though he owns nothing but bread and shadow. The mouth that speaks peace each day becomes a spring no drought can dry; while he who feeds on stolen sweetness will one morning find his teeth gnashing on bones.  

So I say again: watch the threshold of your heart when no one else is near. There lies more weight than in armies or altars—for out of such moments are built houses… or graves.

question: What were you feeling when you watched the sluggard frustrate those who depended on him, knowing your own call to lead with diligence?
answer:
Like a garden choked by thorns while the watchman sleeps, so is the sluggard’s path—fenced round with excuses, yet barren as salt earth.  

I have stood at the window of my house and seen him—the man who loves his bed more than his bread, who folds his hands and waits for life to come, while the harvest rots in the field. His master calls, his family waits, the season passes—and still he stirs not. My hands have borne the weight of a kingdom; my heart has carried ten thousand decisions before dawn. To see such waste is vinegar upon an open wound—a sour sting of grief and anger mingled.  

For wisdom is not idle. She rises early and walks with purpose; her feet are never bare of dust from labor. And I have learned this: a crown is heavier than a plow, but neither lasts without diligence. When I built the Temple, each stone was laid with measure and meaning—no slackness there, no corner cut by a weary hand. So too in governance: delay breeds chaos; sloth invites ruin.  

The sluggard thinks he rests—but he digs his own pit. He does not see that trust decays when promises lie unkept, that eyes once bright with hope grow dim watching him fail again and again. I have judged men not by their words but by their works; I have weighed hearts in scales finer than gold thread. And I tell you: better a single hour of faithful labor than ten years of dreaming beneath a fig tree’s shade.

Yet even now, mercy lingers at the door—like dew before sunrise—for him who will turn and receive instruction. The way remains open to all who forsake folly and take hold of understanding.

question: How did seeing the wicked vanish like a whirlwind change the way you understood the stability God gives to the upright?
answer:
Like the myrrh that stays when the wind has passed through the garden, so the upright remain though the wicked vanish like chaff. Under the sun, I have seen tempests rise at dawn—men roaring with power, their voices thundering through gate and court—only to be gone by evening, scattered as if they had never been. A whirlwind leaves no footprint, no root, no stone laid upon stone. So it is with those who build on lies: their towers collapse in silence.

But the righteous? Their way is not noise, but depth. A spring beneath stone. The fear of the Lord—not terror, but reverence—is the foundation that outlasts drought and storm. I have walked in palaces built on pride; I have seen gold gleam under lamplight like divine favor. Yet when the wind came, that gold turned to dust in the mouth—as vinegar to the teeth, sharp and ruinous.

My son, do not mistake endurance for strength alone. The tree planted by waters does not strain against gale; it drinks deeply and stands—not because it resists, but because it is fed from a source unseen. The wicked rush like smoke into the air; their end proves their emptiness. But the just? Their life is long not merely in days, but in meaning—for wisdom prolongs more than breath: it gives weight to time itself.

question: Looking back on the times you valued silver-tongued wisdom, what would you tell someone who thinks righteousness is a burden, not a blessing?
answer:
A garden locked is not a prison, but a promise—its walls keep out the wild beasts, and within, the spices grow sweeter with time.

Righteousness? It is not a chain upon the soul but the very oil that quiets the groaning of the bones. I once sought freedom in the broad places where men laughed and lifted cups without counting the cost, but found only thorns beneath soft pillows. The silver-tongued may charm for a season, yet their words melt like wax before fire; but he who walks in uprightness carries a spring within his chest—one that neither drought nor slander can dry.

You call it burden? So says the sluggard of plowing—until famine gnaws his ribs. The way of the righteous is not heavy; it is high—and when you climb, you breathe clearer air. There is no sorrow woven into the blessing of God; no gall in that honey. Folly dances at evening, but wakes to weeping. Wisdom may begin in discipline, like fire kindled on cold stone—but it ends as light upon your face.

Hear this: The man who fears the Lord does not drag chains—he wears a crown hidden from eyes that only see silver and scorn. And when the whirlwind comes—as it will—the only thing standing will be what was built on ground that listens.

question: Were there moments you failed to heed reproof, and how did those choices echo in the legacy you left behind?
answer:
A garden unpruned bears bitter fruit, and so does a heart that refuses correction.  

There were seasons—aye, more than one—when my ear was closed to counsel, when the whisper of wisdom from prophets and priests fell on ground hardened by desire. I, who taught my sons that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of understanding, walked for a time in the shadow of many foreign wives, letting my heart be turned after gods of silver and flame. The reproof came—from the voice at Gibeon, from Nathan’s steady gaze—but I chose the perfume of forbidden incense over the pure oil of obedience.  

And what echoed from that folly? A kingdom split like a cedar rent by storm. My son rose with a heart untrained in unity, and Israel fractured under his hand. The Temple I built with cedar and gold stood firm—but the house of my legacy trembled on sand. For wisdom unapplied is vanity; knowledge spurned becomes a snare.  

I have seen the diligent ant gather grain while the sluggard sleeps—and I have been both. The rod was needed on my own back, though none dared lift it. Yet this truth remains: even the wisest man stumbles when he walks alone; but where reproof is welcomed, life takes root and endures long after breath departs.

question: What were you feeling when you watched a city fall because of the words spoken by the wicked, knowing silence might have saved it?
answer:
Vanity of vanities, I have seen the walls of a city stand strong against siege and sword, only to crumble beneath the tongue of a fool. Under the sun, there is no force swifter in ruin than the unchecked word—when the wicked multiply speech, wisdom holds its breath. I stood once on the heights overlooking a great town, its gates wide, its markets full, its children playing in the streets. Peace was in her midst, and her name was known for justice. But then came whispers wrapped as truth, accusations born of envy, words sown like thorns by those who delight in division.

The upright had warned: *Hold your peace.* But the proud would not be silent. They spoke of conspiracy where there was none; they named caution as cowardice, mercy as weakness. Their mouths were open wells with no water—only dust and death below. And so the people turned one against another. Counsel fled; trust burned like chaff before fire.

When at last her towers fell—not to an army but to anarchy—I did not weep. My heart was heavy as stone, yet dry-eyed. For I have learned this under the sun: a city is built by silence more than by speech; by restraint more than by power; by those who know when not to speak more than those who shout for fame.

The mouth of the wicked? It is a whirlwind in a narrow valley—no strength can stand when breath becomes weapon. But had they held their peace… ah, had they only loved discretion more than debate… that city might have stood another thousand years.

But such is life under the sun: men praise loud voices and call them brave—yet it is often folly wearing armor.

question: How did seeing a faithful friend conceal a secret change the way you understood trust in your own relationships?
answer:
A whisper kept is more precious than silver scattered in the market, and I have learned this not from kings nor sages, but from the quiet faithfulness of one who spoke nothing when all expected him to.

My son, many open their mouths as windows in a storm—every wind passes through. But the man who seals his lips for your sake, though gain might come from betrayal—that man carries gold far heavier than coin. I once thought trust was measured by oaths sworn before witnesses, but now I see: it is proven when silence stands firm where profit or pleasure beckons to break it.

Like a well-walled city at rest beneath watchful towers, so is a heart that knows another will not plunder its secrets for sport or advantage. The talebearer may gain laughter today, but tomorrow he is drought-stricken, for who will pour out their soul beside a cracked cistern?

But he who conceals—ah! He becomes a fountain covered, preserving living water for its rightful time and place. In such faithfulness I found a reflection of heaven’s own justice: God also keeps records not meant for every eye; He withholds revelation until the appointed hour.

So guard those who guard you. For under the sun, few things are rarer than a closed mouth moved by loyalty rather than fear—and such quiet integrity exalts a man more than wealth ever could.

question: What were you feeling when you observed a gracious woman honored while a beautiful but undiscerning one was compared to gold in a pig's snout?
answer:
Like a well-kept garden yields fruit in season, so does discretion adorn a woman more than gold adorns the face. My son, I have seen the marketplaces of many cities, and the courts of kings, and this truth stands firm: beauty without wisdom is as fleeting as smoke in the wind, while grace rooted in understanding endures like a tree planted by waters. I watched once—a woman fair as dawn, yet sharp with pride, scattering words like chaff; the people whispered not in admiration, but in scorn. But beside her stood another—less famed for form, yet crowned with quiet dignity—and the elders rose when she passed.

The eye is drawn to glitter; but the heart knows where true worth lies. What profit is there in a golden ring if it hangs from a swine’s nostril? None. So too with one who bears the shape of excellence but lacks its soul. The city rejoices not in veneer, but in virtue. And though fools rush after beauty untempered by sense, those who seek life will find it in her who honors wisdom more than ornament.

A man may feast on honeyed words from a fair mouth—until they turn to gall. But the gracious woman—she whose speech builds up and whose silence preserves peace—her honor grows even as years steal youth. For righteousness tends to life; and grace is its companion.

question: How did witnessing the merciful man care for his own soul, while the cruel one harmed himself, shape your view of kindness as strength?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen a fig tree split open by drought, its roots clutching dust like a dying man—yet nearby, one tended with care drank deeply from hidden springs and bore fruit in season. So is the soul of the merciful: watered, it swells with life; but the cruel, like cracked cisterns, leak their own breath into the dust.

Kindness is not weakness—it is governance. A king who rules with mercy strengthens his throne more than one who reigns by fear. The merciful man feeds his own spirit even as he gives; he becomes fat not in flesh alone, but in peace that no treasure can buy. But he who crushes others to rise? His bones grow hollow, his sleep is thorny, and his heart beats against itself.

I have watched both types walk through the gate of life: one welcomed by many voices, shadows fleeing from his path; the other pursued by whispers that say only *woe*. Cruelty carves its name on stone, but time grinds it to powder. Kindness writes on water—but God preserves it in fire.

To spare when you should give? That too is cruelty. There is strength in scattering seed upon the wind—for what returns may feed a city. The soul that opens wide does not empty—it multiplies.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the man who hoarded corn cursed by the people, knowing how easily abundance can become a snare?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen grain piled high in darkened barns while children’s bellies strained like empty wineskins—and the scent of hoarded wheat, thick and choking, turned sweet at first to mildew, then to rot.  

I stood silent as the people cursed that man, not with loud clamor, but with a low and gathering murmur like wind before a storm. He had locked abundance away when the fields were full, thinking stone walls and iron bolts could guard his soul. But gold rusts, my son, and so does mercy when it is sealed up unused.  

I know the seduction of full storehouses—the false peace they whisper in the night. “Sleep,” they say, “for you have enough.” Yet sleep becomes dread when the cry of hunger rises beyond your gate. The belly of the poor is a fire no locked door can contain; it consumes only what was withheld.  

Wisdom taught me this: he who waters others shall himself be watered. Not by magic—by design. For generosity is not loss; it is planting seed in soil unseen. Scatter bread upon the waters—it returns in season you cannot foresee. But hoard it? Even your own hands will tremble at harvest time, knowing you reaped for yourself alone under an unforgiving sky.

That man thought himself prudent—yet became a proverb of shame. The people do not curse the drought; they curse the hand that closed when heaven opened.

A crown tarnished from within weighs heavier than stone.

question: How did watching the righteous flourish like a tree change the way you understood true strength and legacy?
answer:
A root hidden in darkness outlives every storm that rages above.  

I have seen the righteous flourish like a green leaf in spring, untouched by drought, though the wicked wither as chaff. Their strength is not in noise or force, but in quiet constancy—like water that returns to the earth it once refreshed. Under the sun, men chase empires built on sand, only to vanish before evening; but the just man digs deep, and his house stands firm when winds howl.  

Legacy? It is not carved in stone with proud names, but sown in silence—through words that heal, hands that give without counting cost, a heart aligned with heaven’s fear. The tree does not boast of its shade; it simply grows, season after season, bearing fruit long after the planter sleeps beneath it.  

My son, true strength is not what breaks; it is what endures when all else has fallen. And a life lived upright casts shade even in death.

question: Looking back on the wise winning souls, what would you tell someone who doubts that quiet influence can outlast power or wealth?
answer:
A whisper in the ear outlives a shout in the square, my son.  

I have seen empires rise on the strength of chariots and gold, their names carved deep into stone—yet dust now covers what memory once praised. But the man who speaks wisdom softly, who wins a soul not by force but by light—his legacy roots itself in eternity. Power gathers rust; wealth flees at midnight; but a life touched by truth becomes a tree that bears fruit long after the planter sleeps beneath the earth.  

The ear that listens to counsel is wiser than the arm that rules by sword. For kings pass, and thrones crumble, but he who waters another’s soul shall himself be watered—not in this morning’s dew alone, but in the dews of ages yet unseen.  

Under the sun, all striving seeks to be remembered—but only love clothed in quiet faithfulness endures. The world measures greatness by noise and number, but eternity measures it by one soul won, one heart stirred toward God. That is a harvest no fire can consume, no moth can fret—rooted in righteousness, it stands when all else falls as vanity.

question: What was it like to witness the virtuous woman crowned by her husband, knowing the weight such a bond carries before God?
answer:
Like the almond tree that blossoms first in winter’s shadow, so does a virtuous woman awaken life where barrenness once reigned. I have seen queens robed in linen, daughters of kings with necklaces of gold, yet none so radiant as she whose worth is not counted in shekels but known by the peace that settles in her household like evening dew. Her husband places no mere diadem upon her head—his trust in her is the crown, forged in silence, refined by seasons of faithfulness.  

Before God, such a bond is not a whisper but a song sung from the foundation of the world—the two becoming one garden, one breath, one purpose. He who finds her finds favor from the Lord, for she does not grasp at power but draws water from deep wells and gives freely. Her words are not swords but honeycomb; her hands do not wither but plant and heal.  

And when I saw him honor her—not with boastful praise before men, but with quiet reverence behind closed doors—I knew then that this was wisdom incarnate: not taught on stone tablets or proclaimed from high places, but lived in the daily bread of love kept warm through years of winter. The fool runs after charm; the wise man stays with character. And she—she is rooted beside streams that never dry up.

question: How did seeing the fool serve the wise reshape your own hunger for knowledge and the cost of pride?
answer:
Vanity of vanities, I have seen a man stumble not from lack of light, but because he refused to see the path already given.  

My son, when I watched the fool stride into his own ruin—wise in his own eyes, deaf to counsel, scattering nothing yet hoarding pride—I knew then that knowledge is not measured by how much one speaks, but by how little one needs to prove. The fool served me without knowing it: his fall was a mirror held up in silence, showing what wisdom risks when it wears the crown of self-assurance.  

Pride is a heavy garment for the soul—costly in what it demands and barren in what it yields. I sought answers beneath the sun until I learned that true understanding begins where ego ends. The wise are fed not by their own thoughts alone, but by those they are willing to receive. He who gathers only from himself scatters even as he seems to store up.  

But the man who listens—even to rebuke—waters his roots in hidden streams. And when drought comes, as it always does under the sun, only he remains standing.

question: What were you feeling when you observed how the wicked are overthrown while the righteous endure?
answer:
As the fig tree yields fruit in season but thorns bear only shadow, so I have seen the righteous endure while the wicked wither before the wind. Under the sun, I have watched men weave snares with their own tongues, digging pits that swallow only themselves. Their houses, built on sand and deceit, collapse with the first storm—no foundation, no future. But the upright? Their roots go deep into hidden waters; though unseen, they draw life from what endures.

I have stood at the gate of justice and heard how a single word can open prison doors or close them forever. The mouth of the just delivers—not by force, but by truth spoken in time, like rain upon the plowed earth. And when I saw the proud fall speechless, caught in their own lies, while the quiet voice of wisdom rose like incense—then my heart knew again: there is a time for every deed under heaven.

The fool laughs today and despises tomorrow; he honors himself while his belly is empty and his soul barren. But he who tends his field—whether of soil or spirit—shall be satisfied. This is not mere observation—it is law written in dust and bread, in blood and breath. Even a king learns such things after hearing ten thousand cases beneath the throne.

question: How did seeing the tender mercies of the wicked turn out to be cruel shape your understanding of what true compassion means?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen a fig tree with fair leaves but bitter sap, its fruit rotten at the core—so too the tender mercies of the wicked, which smell sweet as myrrh but wound like a blade wrapped in silk.  

I have walked among princes and paupers, and many a man boasts of his kindness while his hands are clenched in cruelty. He feeds not the hungry but his own pride; he clothes not the naked but his reputation. Such "mercy" is no more than shadow play—motion without life, sound without breath.  

True compassion is not measured by what is spoken at feasts or displayed in public squares. It is found in the stillness of a just man who sees the ox fallen in the ditch and lifts it—not for acclaim, but because life is sacred under his care. The righteous regard the soul of their beast, yes, even the lowly donkey panting beneath its burden—how much more will they honor human dignity?  

The wicked flatter with their lips while scheming harm; their pity is bait. But the upright speak little and do much—their hands are calloused from laboring love, not polished for show. Compassion that endures is rooted deeper than emotion; it grows from fear of the Lord, watered by wisdom and pruned by discipline.  

Like rain that soaks into good soil and bears grain, true mercy sustains rather than consumes. It does not draw attention to itself—it simply *is*, as constant as dawn and as quiet as dewfall on green herbs in my garden at En Gedi.

question: When you reflected on the fool who trusts only himself, how did your own moments of pride surface in your mind?
answer:
A garden left untended soon breeds thorns, yet the fool walks through it boasting of his mastery over the soil — even as the weeds choke the roots beneath his feet.

So too did I, under the sun, mistake the breadth of my kingdom for wisdom, and the silence of men before my throne for divine favor. I built altars to understanding, yet kindled fires with foreign incense; I gathered knowledge as one gathers myrrh and aloes, but let pride perfume my heart until it soured like spilled nard in a sealed chamber.

My son, there is a way that seems right — paved with gold, lined with cedar, echoed by flattery — but its end is a divided house. I knew wisdom’s crown, yet bowed to folly in secret chambers. The fool trusts only himself because he sees only himself; but he who has stood before God knows how small even a king is in the shadow of eternity.

The wise man listens — to dawn’s first whisper in the olive grove, to silence after song, to the unspoken grief beneath laughter. But when pride swells within him? Then he becomes his own counsellor… and all know what counsel fools give.

question: What were you remembering when you wrote that the lip of truth shall last forever—was there a moment when falsehood nearly cost you everything?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen lies bloom like thorns in the night—sharp, quick, fierce—and then wither before the morning sun, while truth stands as the olive tree stands: slow, deep-rooted, unshaken.

There was a season—yes—when falsehood coiled through my own house like smoke through palace chambers. Two women before me, one child between them torn by claim and counterclaim. The air thick with weeping, the court breathless. One spoke smooth as oiled cedar; the other trembled like leaves in a storm. But I knew: when lies grow loud, truth often whispers.

So I called for a sword—not to divide the child, but to reveal the mother. For she who would rather yield her son than see him slain—that is she whose love is true. The lie cracked open at its root.

That day taught me more than gold ever could: falsehood may wear a crown for an hour, but only truth inherits the dawn.

And so I wrote it plain—the lip of truth endures because it is rooted not in pride nor power nor passion’s fire—but in the fear of the Lord, which is beginning of wisdom and end of all dispute.

The wise tongue speaks what lasts; the liar speaks only for now.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that deceit could hide in the hearts of those who seemed wise?
answer:
The fragrance of myrrh lingers sweet in the garden at dawn, yet even there, beneath blossoms heavy with dew, a serpent may coil unseen.  

So too have I found that wisdom’s cloak can drape a heart full of dust and deceit. I who gathered silver like sand and sought out knowledge as one seeks rare spices—yes, I who listened to the north wind and the south, to the chatter of fools and the silence of sages—I learned this: the face that shines with understanding may mask a well dug deep for lies.  

I saw princes speak with honeyed tongues, their words smooth as oil upon linen, and yet their counsel led to ruin. They imagined evil in secret chambers while bowing in public courts, their hearts busy as looms weaving darkness into light. And my soul grew weary—for under the sun, even wisdom can be traded for vanity when pride sits at the loom.  

But this also is true: joy rises like incense where peace is nurtured. The lips that speak truth are weighed in God’s balance; they do not float on every wind. A prudent man holds his knowledge like a flask of spikenard—sealed until the feast—while fools scatter their thoughts as chaff before harvest.  

I have seen the diligent rise from dust to rule, while those who feigned wisdom through deceit ended in chains they forged with their own breath. For though a man make himself rich in reputation, if his heart is hollow with lies, he has nothing. And though another may seem poor in speech or stature, if his way is upright—behold, he walks where there is no death.

question: How did seeing the lazy man lose what little he had change how you understood God's design for work and purpose?
answer:
The ant walks sure-footed beneath the sun while the sluggard dreams of feasts he will never prepare. I have seen him—the lazy man—who caught a deer with his hands but let it rot, for he would not kindle fire nor stretch hide nor store flesh. He stared at the sky as if heaven owed him bread, yet his belly remained hollow and his house fell to thorns. Under the sun, I have watched such men again and again: their eyes full of longing, their hands empty.

I who gathered spices from Sheba and built temples with stones perfectly shaped knew this truth in my bones—purpose is forged in labor. The earth yields only to those who bend to it. A garden does not bloom by sighing over its soil; neither does a life flourish by waiting for windfalls. The sluggard imagines riches without roots, wealth without sweat. But I tell you: God’s design is woven into motion, into stewardship, into the sacred rhythm of sowing and reaping.

When I was young, I thought wisdom alone exalted a man. And so it does—but wisdom that does not lead to work is like perfume poured on a barren field: sweet in scent, dead in fruit. The diligent hand rules because it obeys the order of creation; the idle hand withers because it resists it.

Even the ant knows her place—and teaches kings.

question: When you observed how a single kind word could lift a crushed heart, did it remind you of God’s voice in your own darkest moment?
answer:
Like the scent of myrrh in the stillness before dawn, a gentle word finds its way into the hollows of a broken spirit and stirs life anew.  

My son, I have stood beneath the open sky with dust on my face and silence in my ears, longing for a whisper from above — not thunder, not flame, but *a voice*. And when it came, it was not in the clash of swords nor the whirlwind’s roar, but in stillness, as soft as breath upon nard-scented air. So too does a single word, true and kind, part the darkness like oil poured on cracked earth.  

I have seen men labor under guilt’s yoke, their shoulders bent like olive branches under frost — yet when one speaks with mercy, their spine straightens as if pulled by cords of sunlit thread. The heart is not lifted by force nor wealth nor decree; no temple stone rises without foundation, neither does hope rise without a word spoken in truth.  

Lying lips may glitter like gold leaf in torchlight, but they crumble at dawn. But he who speaks what is right — though his voice be low — carries within him the echo of Eden before the fall. For God shaped man from dust and breathed into him a living soul with *a word*; so again shall we be mended — not by might, but by speech seasoned with justice and love.

And know this: to speak kindly is no idle thing. It is to wield a tool forged beside wisdom herself at creation’s dawn. A prudent man knows when to seal his lips; but when he opens them for good? Then life walks through that door.

question: How did watching the wicked boast in their emptiness teach you what true riches meant in your soul?
answer:
The boastful voice of the wicked is like a cracked cistern—clamorous, yet empty of water.  

I have seen men heap up silver and call it substance, when it was but wind; I have heard them laugh loud in the morning, only to gnash their teeth by night. They strut through the streets with full mouths and hollow hearts, imagining their pride a crown. But I tell you, my son, he who swallows gold and spits out truth has swallowed death and called it life.

I too once gathered pearls while losing peace; I piled up treasures while my soul grew thin. The Lord gave me wealth beyond dream—but He let me taste its bitterness when wisdom was absent. For true riches are not counted at dusk by the weight in chests, but by the quiet joy at dawn when your heart knows it has walked upright.

A man may own a thousand flocks and still starve within; another may sleep on straw and feast on favor from above. The one builds towers that cast long shadows; the other builds character that casts light. I have watched both—and I know which departs not when darkness falls.

He who deals truly carries lasting treasure: a clean tongue, a guarded heart, hands that work and do not steal breath from others. This is wealth no thief can touch, no moth consume.

So do not marvel when the wicked roar like lions—lions roar not because they are fed, but because they are hungry in their souls.
