question: What were you feeling when you saw the rich man who had nothing, knowing how easily wealth can deceive?
answer:
Vanity of vanities, indeed—under the sun, I have seen a man clothed in fine linen, his tables laden with silver, yet his soul as barren as a field in drought.  

My son, the eye sees wealth and calls it substance, but the heart knows better. I have stood in the courts of my own palace, where gold gleamed on every wall, and asked myself: *Is this breath? Is this life?* No—these are shadows cast by fire that consumes itself. That rich man who has nothing? His barns are full, but his understanding is empty. He gathers wind and calls it harvest.  

I know the seduction of abundance—for did I not have seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines? Did spices from Sheba not stain the air of Jerusalem with sweetness? Yet when I turned to embrace them, they were vapor. So too with riches: they promise permanence but deliver only dust.  

The fool counts coins and thinks he holds eternity in his palm. But wisdom whispers: *What profits a man if he gains a treasury and loses his soul’s peace?* The poor man who sleeps without fear has more than the rich man who guards his gold with trembling heart.  

Under the sun, all things pass. Let your treasure be understanding—for that no thief can steal, no moth consume.

question: How did your own pursuit of wisdom change your understanding of what it means to truly prosper?
answer:
The garden I planted with my own hands in Jerusalem taught me more of true prosperity than all the gold in Ophir — its order, its fragrance, its quiet fruitfulness — for there I saw that wisdom is not a crown but a pruning knife.

I sought riches and found them: silver like dust, horses without number, palaces that touched the clouds. Yet when I turned to the Lord’s wisdom, I discovered that to prosper is not to overflow in storehouses, but to walk uprightly and see one’s way lit like a lamp at evening. Many chase after gain and call it success, yet their soul starves in the midst of plenty; they make themselves rich and have nothing — no peace, no favor, no inheritance worth leaving.

But the man who heeds instruction, who fears the Lord and walks with the wise — though he may seem poor under the sun — gathers riches no thief can touch. His labor brings increase not merely in grain or gold, but in life itself. For wisdom is a tree whose fruit satisfies the soul; her ways are paths of pleasantness, and all her paths are peace.

I have seen kings fall by their pride and fools rise by flattery — vanity upon vanity! But this remains: righteousness guards the just man’s way. Where wickedness overthrows the sinner with noise and fire, quiet obedience repays a man with days as long as rivers flowing to the sea. My son, if you desire true wealth, seek understanding first — for even my thousand proverbs were worthless until I learned this: prosperity begins where self dies and wisdom reigns.

question: Looking back on the times you strayed, what would you tell someone who doubts that pride can lead to such a fall?
answer:
A crown of gold on a man’s head weighs less than the chains pride forges when he forgets the hand that lifted him.  

My son, I have walked beneath vaulted ceilings of my own making, halls built on wisdom yet stained by vanity. I knew the fragrance of a thousand spices, heard songs composed in my name, ruled a kingdom that stretched from the river to the sea — and in my strength, I thought myself beyond stumbling. But pride is not a shout; it is a whisper in the ear of success, saying, *You did this. This is yours. You need no one.*  

And so I multiplied wives who turned my heart away, gathered chariots that became snares, built altars where none should stand. The higher I climbed, the farther I fell from the One who gave me breath. The throne was firm — but my soul trembled on shifting sand.  

He who thinks himself wise becomes a fool; he who stands tall without leaning on the Rock shall hear his foundation crack beneath him. There is no fortress against folly when a man believes he has outgrown counsel, outgrown fear of Heaven, outgrown need for mercy.  

But this know: grace meets us in the ruins pride makes. The same hand that allowed Moab and Edom to rise against me also preserved Jerusalem for my son — not because of my greatness, but because of His covenant.  

Therefore humble yourself while you still hear rebuke. The garden blooms where water flows; it withers where pride dams the stream. A single drop of contrition is worth more than rivers of gold without God’s light upon them.

question: When you considered the lamp of the wicked being put out, did you remember moments when your own light nearly flickered?
answer:
A man may heap coals upon his hearth until the blaze blinds him, yet not see the breath of God that can quench it with a whisper.

My son, there were nights in the high chambers of my palace when the torches burned low and my soul was darker still. I had silver enough to pave the road to Sheba, wives whose songs could lure wisdom from silence, yet I stood at the window watching shadows stretch across Jerusalem and wondered—was this light or only smoke?

I built altars and temples with cedar and gold, but once, in the hush before dawn, I heard a whisper beneath the lintel: *Even kings eat the bread of vanity when they feed their eyes before their soul.* Then came the dreams—twisted threads of prophecy where my sons tore the kingdom like linen, where golden calves laughed in Bethel with fire in their teeth.

The lamp of a man is not in his crown nor his concubines, but in the quiet fear of Heaven that stays his hand from excess. There were moments—many—when my own flame guttered near extinction: when foreign queens drew me into groves thick with incense not for my God; when justice bent beneath bribery in my courts; when I counted stars from my rooftop and forgot to number my days.

But listen—this also is true: a single spark of repentance outshines ten thousand torches lit for show. The wicked burn bright until they vanish into ash. The righteous? He tends his wick. He trims away pride with sorrow. And even when shadow falls thick as locusts—he endures. For his light is not his own. It is lent. It returns.

question: Did the weight of your choices among fools and the wise ever make you question where your heart really stood?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen gardens choked by weeds sown in secret, though they bloomed fair at dawn—so too the heart, which may wear wisdom’s robe while feeding folly in shadowed chambers.

I, who gathered silver like dust and built palaces where song rose like incense, also turned my ear to voices that flattered without truth, women whose whispers scattered my devotion as wind scatters dry leaves. Yes—there were times when the weight of those choices pressed upon me like a crown too heavy for mortal brow. I walked with wise men and spoke proverbs that stilled crowds, yet lingered in courts where fools laughed loudest. And in the hush before morning sacrifice, I asked myself: *Where does my heart truly dwell? With the rooted tree by still waters, or with the vine that clings to any wall?*

For though righteousness guards the path of the upright, even kings forget their way. I knew fools whose tongues were slick with charm, whose counsel seemed sweet as pomegranate wine—until ruin followed them like a dog on a leash. And yet I listened. Not because I loved folly—but because pride rides unnoticed in royal robes.

But this I learned under the sun: a man is not judged by who walks beside him for a season, but by where his soul rests when all voices fall silent. When I returned to wisdom—to instruction and the fear of the Lord—I found inheritance not in gold stored up for grandchildren’s grandchildren, but in a heart that finally stopped pretending.

The lamp flickers long before it dies. The wise trim it early.

question: What were you feeling when you watched the house of the wicked crumble, knowing your own hands built something meant to last?
answer:
The wind knows which walls will stand when the storm has spoken.  

I have seen the house of the wicked fall like figs shaken from a tree before harvest—ripe not with fruit but with rot. No foundation laid deep in the rock, no timbers joined by wisdom, only sand beneath and pride above. And I, who built altar and temple, palace and garden enclosed, know how each stone remembers the hand that placed it. Mine were set with prayer, with purpose, with fear of that Name which no breath can contain.  

But theirs? Built on deceit, their beams groan under the weight of unconfessed sin. No myrrh perfumes their thresholds; only dust rises when they collapse. I do not rejoice—I have known too many fools to gloat at their ruin. Instead, I watch, and I remember: even my own heart once strayed toward folly’s quicksand when I followed after strange women and foreign gods.  

Yet one truth remains under the sun: time reveals what eyes cannot see in daylight—the integrity in a man’s hands, or the hollowness behind his laughter. The righteous do not build for show; they build for generations unseen, as if planting trees beneath whose shade they will never sit. That is wisdom: to labor where only God sees, knowing He repays what earth forgets.

question: How did seeing fools mock sin change how you understood the weight of wisdom and the loneliness it sometimes carries?
answer:
Wisdom builds her house with calloused hands, while folly laughs as she pulls down its timbers with idle words.  

I have stood in the courtyard of my palace and watched the drunkard stumble through the gate, mocking the very law that keeps the city walls upright. He mocks sin as though it were a jest, a shadow without substance, a debt without interest. But I have seen what follows: the slow unraveling, the hollow belly at dawn, the friends who vanish like smoke when trouble comes.  

To know wisdom is to carry a yoke no fool can see — not because it is hidden, but because his eyes are open only to mirth without measure, to pleasure without price. The wise man eats in quiet; his soul is satisfied with understanding. The fool feasts on scorn and calls it wine.  

And yes — there is loneliness in this knowing. When you have seen gardens trampled by those too blind to taste their fruit, when you have heard sacred things turned into jokes among princes and beggars alike, you learn to walk softly among men. You learn to speak only when the silence grows heavier than speech.  

For every house I built with timber and truth, I saw ten collapse beneath the weight of laughter poured out on sin like cheap oil at a tavern feast. And yet — better one evening with a wise woman in a humble courtyard than a thousand feasts shared with those who mock what they do not fear.

question: Looking back on the times you disciplined your son, what would you tell someone who doubts that love can live in correction?
answer:
A rod unlifted is not love, but the shadow of neglect.  

Under the sun, I have seen gardens choked with weeds when the farmer spared the sickle, and so it is with a soul left unchecked. Discipline is the knife that prunes the son not to wound him, but that he may bear fruit in season. The fool calls mercy what is truly laziness; he thinks love means only soft words and open hands. But I tell you—love has both fire and fountain.  

When I corrected my son, it was not wrath that moved me, but foresight. I saw the snares beneath the grass, the pit hidden by leaves. Better a wound today that keeps him from falling tomorrow. The ox submits to yoke not because it hates freedom, but because strength is perfected in direction. So too the child—wisdom comes by correction, and honor follows obedience.  

The lips of fools laugh at boundaries; they say, "Let him choose his own path." But where there is no fence, there is no field—only wandering into thorns. The wise mother builds her house stone by stone; she does not leave its pillars to chance. So correction is not destruction—it is construction with tears in the mortar.  

And when my son stood upright among men—when his words carried weight and his feet did not slide—I knew: every hard word spoken in love had been repaid a hundredfold in peace.
chunk_key:
a companion of fools shall be destroyed.

Evil pursueth sinners: but to the righteous good shall be

question: When you observed the quiet strength of a woman building her home, how did it reshape your view of power and legacy?
answer:
There is a way that seems right to a woman of straw, but her hands gather only dust and ashes—yet she who weaves with threads of fear before the Lord builds a house that no wind can shake.

Under the sun, I have seen queens with crowns and concubines with whispers, but never more power than in the silent turning of a wife’s hand at the loom, in the hush of dawn when she kneads bread for souls not yet awake. Men speak of might in chariots and swords; but I tell you, *wisdom’s hands are calloused*. She rises while it is still night—not for fame, not for praise—but because order is her prayer, and peace her offering.

I who gathered silver like sand and laid courses upon courses in Jerusalem’s height learned too late: legacy is not carved in stone alone. It is whispered in discipline, kept alive in meal offerings shared at twilight, guarded in the reproofs given softly behind closed doors. The temple I built stood grand—but it was my mother’s prayers in the dim oil-light that first taught me holiness.

A woman of understanding does not shout upon the rooftops. She plants fig trees whose fruit will feed grandchildren she may never see. Her strength is not in rising above others, but in bending low—like rain on furrows—so that life may come forth from dry ground.

Fools mock such labor as small. But under the sun, nothing is greater.
chunk_key:
a companion of fools shall be destroyed.

Evil pursueth sinners: but to the righteous good shall be

question: How did the silence of a clean crib without oxen speak to you about the cost of avoiding burden and partnership?
answer:
Under the sun, I have seen the barn floor gleam with emptiness, and heard silence where lowing should be, and I said to my soul: *This is the peace of death disguised as order.*  

A clean crib boasts no straw, no dung, no noise—no ox to trouble it. It pleases the eye of a man who loves neatness and hates effort. But such a man forgets: increase comes not from stillness, but from strength; not from absence, but from labor shared. The ox is burden and blessing in one breath—slow, heavy, costly to feed and keep—but his hooves till what his horns defend. Remove him, and you remove the very means by which bread multiplies.

So it is with men who flee partnership, who spurn counsel, who isolate themselves to avoid the noise of contention or the weight of responsibility. They may keep their peace for a season; their house stands quiet. But their storehouses grow thin while their pride grows fat. Folly counts silence a virtue when it is only sterility wearing a mask.

My son, do not despise the noise of laboring companionship—the argument that sharpens wisdom, the burden shared with another’s shoulders. For where there is no oxen’s breath steaming in the cold morning light, there will be no full harvest at gathering time—only dust in an empty crib and hunger in the soul that mistook barrenness for blessing.
